items.title: Ukraine strikes Russian oil refinery near Moscow following drone attacks | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Ukraine says it has struck a Russian oil refinery near Moscow in retaliation for a wave of drone attacks on Kyiv and other cities. At least seven people were killed in the Ukrainian capital when Russian drones targeted power and water infrastructure, as well as residential areas. The UN Secretary General Antonio Guterres has condemned the attacks as violations of  international law. 

Chapters:

2:59 Mark Cancian, Center for Strategic & International Studies

items.title: Cliff Asness on how #AI changes quantitative investing #stockmarket #shorts
items.author: Bloomberg Television
items.content:
<article>On this episode of the Odd Lots podcast, Clifford Asness, co-founder and CEO of AQR Capital Management, joins Tracy Alloway and Joe Weisenthal to talk about the history of quantitative investing, market efficiency and the emergence of AI/ML in his process http://apple.co/4lTgFT6

The Odd Lots podcast has been around for 10 years. Unfortunately, markets have gotten less rational over the same time frame. At least this is the contention of Cliff Asness, the co-founder and CEO of AQR Capital Management, a quantitative investing firm that's been around for nearly three decades. Asness' approach to investing is rooted in academic theory, having studied under the legendary Eugene Fama at the University of Chicago. In the world of social media and meme stocks, it's tough out there for the academically minded. And that's forced Cliff to adjust his approach over time. On this episode, we talk about the history of quantitative investing, market efficiency, and the emergence of AI/ML in his process. We also talk about the reality of investing other people's money, and the challenge of sticking with one's convictions at a time when temporary forces are working against you.</article>

items.title: Trump to sue BBC despite apology over edit
items.author: Minnie Stephenson
items.content:
The BBC has apologised to Donald Trump, over the way one of his speeches was edited, and retracted the Panorama episode involved.

But that wasn’t enough for the US president, who says he’s determined to launch a multi billion dollar lawsuit. It’s not the first time Mr Trump has sued a media outlet of course, but it’s not entirely clear whether an overseas legal case would succeed.

items.title: Trump signals that US is likely to scale up military action against Venezuela | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Donald Trump has signalled that the US is likely to scale up military action against Venezuela targeting alleged drug smuggling. A US task force in the Caribbean is expanding rapidly, and has carried out 20 strikes on suspected drug trafficking boats in the region. Donald Trump has threatened to expand these attacks onto Venezuelan soil, accusing President Nicolas Maduro of running a drug cartel. On board Air Force One, reporters pressed Trump on his plans.

items.title: Beyond AI: Inside the Global Quantum Computer Race
items.author: Bloomberg Television
items.content:
<article>Einstein doubted it. But nearly a century later, quantum mechanics is driving one of the biggest technology races of our time. From drug discovery to finance and cybersecurity, the quantum revolution may redefine how we compute, invest, and live, and it could even outperform AI. IBM’s Jamie Garcia and Jerry Chow explain why 2029 is the year to watch for quantum, while IonQ’s Niccolo de Masi says his company is already years ahead.
--------

items.title: Jon Gray Is Reshaping Blackstone for the Retail Crowd
items.author: Bloomberg Television
items.content:
<article>From viral jogging videos to advocating for new investment approaches, Blackstone President and COO Jon Gray is transforming a private equity giant built on exclusivity.

Blackstone, once a fortress for the world’s richest institutions, is remaking itself under Gray into a financial superstore for the dentist, lawyer and teacher next door. As Chief Executive Officer Steve Schwarzman steps back from much of the day-to-day management of the $1.2 trillion firm he built into a private equity giant, his heir apparent is driving a transformation focused on brand polish and popular appeal.

Read more: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2025-11-12/blackstone-s-jon-gray-opens-up-private-equity-to-the-retail-investing-masses</article>

items.title: Wall Street Week | Santander’s Ana Botín, Quantum Future, Pension Reform, Data Center Win-Win
items.author: Bloomberg Television
items.content:
<article>This week, Santander’s Ana Botín is steering one of Europe’s biggest banks through red tape and rising taxes. She says that growth, not regulation, is what Europe needs most. And, quantum mechanics is driving a multibillion-dollar race. The technology is already in use, but measuring success is the next challenge for investors. Plus, the Netherlands is offering a blueprint for how pension systems around the world adapt. Later, can Finland’s plan to turn data center heat into clean power work worldwide?

items.author: DW News
items.content:

They say the detonation happened in the city of Srinagar in Indian-administered Kashmir. Police and forensic team members were handling confiscated explosives when the material accidentally blew up. It came just days after 13 people were killed in a blast in New Delhi. DW's New Delhi Bureau Chief Sandra Petersmann reports.

items.title: Can Korea's Digital Comics Giant Finally Win Over America?
items.author: Bloomberg Television
items.content: <article>Webtoon Entertainment, the company behind the global webcomic phenomenon, pioneered the vertical-scroll format and drew in more than 150 million users worldwide. It completely dominated South Korea and Japan and its partnership with Disney sent its stock soaring 80%. But is it enough to win over the US market? Sohee Kim reports.</article>

items.title: Returning to Syria: Between loss and hope | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Since the toppling of President Bashar al-Assad in December 2024, Syrian refugees and the internally displaced are gradually returning to their homes – and encountering huge challenges in the process. Returning is difficult as many homes have been destroyed, the infrastructure is ailing and the nation’s recovery is being hampered by a severe economic crisis. Last but not least, the country’s political outlook is uncertain. We meet three individuals who have nevertheless taken the courageous decision to go back home:
Fatima Zahraa came back to Aleppo to raise her children in a familiar culture, while Amr Yakan gave up a secure life in Canada to rebuild his ancestors’ mosque. After losing his family in the 2023 earthquake, filmmaker Fadi Al-Khatib returned to Damascus in 2023, determined to continue realizing his brother’s dream. Despite the destruction and deprivation, these Syrians choose hope over exile and are rebuilding not only houses, but also their identity. Their stories illustrate the emotional complexity involved in returning to a country still etched by conflict. A reportage by Mohamad Chreyteh.

Chapters: 
00:00 Intro 
01:10 Fatima returns from Germany   
03:37 A new hope for Aleppo?   
04:53 Amr feels a sense of duty 
07:57 Devastation in Damascus 
08:30 A new start for Fadi in Damascus

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Unreported World: Inside Arab communities in Israel with soaring unsolved murder rates
items.author: Krishnan Guru-Murthy
items.content:
Israel? That was my first reaction when they told me I was going there for this series.

But of course there is a great deal going on in Israel that is largely unreported and deeply shocking.

Just a brief word on terminology: When we speak of Arab-Israelis we are talking mostly about Palestinians, people who would have been citizens of Mandatory Palestine as it was, who are mostly Muslim, with a minority of Christians and Druze as well as Bedouin in areas like the Negev. Many prefer to call themselves Palestinian Citizens of Israel but in East Jerusalem most are not citizens of Israel and do not wish to be, but are resident.

Allegations of corruption and negligence

Knowing nods, and brief comments of ‘ah yes, it’s terrible’ came from everyone who asked what we were doing.

And many argue this is not just some tragic reality of crime-ridden neighbourhoods. They claim it is a product of deliberate negligence by the far-right Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir who is in charge of the police.

Add to that allegations that many of the weapons being used by Arab Israeli criminals are coming from corruption in the police and military.

Disparity in murder ‘solved’ rates

The numbers tell a story straight away: while around 75% of murders are solved in Israel’s Jewish communities, it is around 15% in Arab areas.

Arabs make up around 20% of Israel’s population but account for more than 70% of the murder victims, with more than 220 murders so far this year.

Arabs make up around 20% of Israel’s population but account for more than 70% of the murder victims, with more than 220 murders so far this year. So what is going on and why is there such a disparity of police success in different communities?

An ‘epidemic’ of murders and shooting

Firstly, it is clear there is an epidemic of murders and shootings going on in many Arab areas.

Many are gang related, with their roots in organised crime, related to extortion of businesses, drugs, guns and car theft.

Some follow a tit-for-tat pattern between families – where one killing leads to revenge attacks. And some are just the result of what happens when access to guns is easy for people who want to settle disputes.

Almost every day we were filming this story in Israel there was a shooting, stabbing or murder we could have reported on.

We focussed on the small coastal town of Jisr as-Zarqa, population around 15,000, where three murders took place in the space of a few days.

And it is where the families all had something in common – beyond their grief and insistence that their young men had nothing to do with crime – ask whether the police will solve their case and they almost laugh through the tears.

I met Mohamed, whose brother Qusay had been shot dead a couple of days earlier while sitting outside the house next to the falafel kiosk the family owns.

On the evening of his killing, the police had stopped and warned him to go inside as they had heard something was going to happen.

But they didn’t stay and Qusay didn’t listen – and he was shot by masked gunmen who drove past, all caught on CCTV.

Mohamed said he held his brother as he died, waiting for the police and ambulance to arrive, saying he couldn’t breathe.

Hopeless task for activists

There are activists trying to keep young men away from the gangs, trying to teach them traditional skills for a coastal town like fishing and boat skills, practices that historically earned young Palestinian men a living but have largely been lost.

But there’s a real sense of them facing a pretty hopeless task – yes, teach a man to fish and he’ll eat for the rest of his life, but teach him to sell drugs or join the gangs and he’ll make enough money to drive around in a flash car and enjoy the good life for as long as he stays alive.

The Israeli police were going to give us an interview to discuss the issue, but at the last minute our filming with them was cancelled.

It is alluring for some in communities where jobs and opportunities are scarce, and hope in short supply.

The Israeli police were going to give us an interview to discuss the issue, but at the last minute our filming with them was cancelled.

They insist they are doing their utmost to tackle crime and that Arab Israeli communities don’t always cooperate.

But Palestinian politicians tell a different story – believing their communities are being left to descend into chaos on purpose and that many young people are choosing to leave Israel altogether as a result.

That, they say, is what Ben-Gvir wants to happen. The Israeli government and Ben-Gvir did not respond to our requests for an interview.

Unreported World: Special Substack series

Each newsletter article – written by our award-winning journalists – will examine and reflect on the extraordinary episodes bringing unique insight.

items.title: Is Trump’s new Epstein ties investigation ‘an act of hope’ or ‘desperation’? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Donald Trump says he has asked the Justice Department to investigate convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein’s ties to many other high-profile figures.  In a post on Truth Social, the US President said he would ask Attorney General Pam Bondi and the FBI to look into Epstein's links to top Democrats -- including former President Bill Clinton. His request follows the release by Congress of emails raising new questions about President Trump's ties to the convicted sex offender. Bondi has confirmed she will launch the investigation on Trump’s orders. 

00:00 Trump asks Justice Department to investigate Epstein ties with other high-profile people. 
01:04  Michael A. Genovese, Professor of Political Science at Loyola Marymount University in Los Angeles. 
05:34 Timothy Hagle is Associate Professor of Political Science at the University of Iowa.

#epsteinfiles #trump #billclinton

items.title: Is Russia stockpiling weapons to overwhelm Ukraine’s defense at one go? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>On Thursday night-Friday morning, Russia launched a massive overnight attack on Kyiv, using hundreds of drones and at least eighteen missiles. Multiple residential buildings across the Ukrainian capital were hit, with emergency services reporting multiple deaths and injuries. Ukrainian authorities say this is the biggest strike on Kyiv in almost three weeks, and repeated their calls to allies to supply more air-defence systems. 

00:00 Russia strikes Kyiv in large-scale overnight attack
03:11 Military analyst Marina Miron from King's College London. 
11:05 Domitilla Sagramoso, Senior Lecturer in Security and Development at King's College London. 
15:08 DW correspondent Nick Connolly

items.title: Regulation Is Holding Back Europe’s Banks, Santander’s Botín Says
items.author: Bloomberg Television
items.content:
<article>Santander Executive Chair Ana Botín discusses the bank’s record year, but cautions that Europe’s regulatory burden is holding back growth. Botín explains how Santander’s global expansion gives it an edge, even as European banks struggle with slower reform and higher taxes. She is a longtime proponent of capital markets integration in Europe, but even she isn’t confident that it will happen any time soon.
--------

items.title: Is The United States Potentially Preparing to Enter a War With Venezuela?
items.author: Bloomberg Television
items.content:
<article>Executive Director of Arizona State University's McCain Institute, Evelyn Farka, shares her thoughts on whether or not the US is preparing to enter a war with Venezuela as the USS Gerald R. Ford is in the region & the US continues to strike boats allegedly smuggling drugs from Venezuela. Farkas states these actions are about regime change and talks about if these strikes will scare & intimidate Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro out of office. She speaks with Joe Mathieu and Tyler Kendall on the late edition of Bloomberg's "Balance of Power."
--------
